beats.text: A scalar autoregression explains one series by its own past, but interest rates, output and inflation move together.
A Vector Autoregression stacks K series into a vector and lets every component depend on the recent past of every component.

visual.tex: y_t = c + A_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + A_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t

beats.text: The off-diagonal coefficients are the whole point: a nonzero entry means yesterday's second series helps predict today's first.
A VAR(p) on K variables carries K times Kp plus one parameters in the mean, and that count explodes as K and p grow.

visual.label: params in a VAR(4) on 8 variables

beats.text: Folding the VAR into companion form, the system is stable exactly when every eigenvalue of F lies inside the unit circle.
In a stable VAR shocks decay geometrically, and the Wold rewrite gives impulse-response matrices that for a VAR(1) are simply powers of A.
Because every equation of a reduced-form VAR shares the identical regressors, equation-by-equation OLS is the maximum-likelihood estimator.

visual.sub: One matrix solve fits the VAR

beats.text: A single multivariate least-squares solve recovers the intercept and every lag coefficient matrix at once.
The hard part is order selection: too few lags leave autocorrelated residuals, too many burn degrees of freedom on noise.
Information criteria add a complexity penalty to the log-likelihood, then you pick the lag that minimizes the score.
BIC's penalty is harshest and consistent, AIC's is mild and over-fits, and Hannan-Quinn sits between the two.
The curse of dimensionality is real, which pushes practitioners toward Bayesian shrinkage priors and factor-augmented VARs.

visual.label: parameters vs a short quarterly sample

beats.text: A fitted VAR is a dense block of coefficients that almost no one can read directly off the page.
The impulse-response function tracks how the whole system reacts over time to a single one-off shock.

visual.note: VAR(1) responses are powers of A

beats.text: For the worked example a unit shock to variable one traces one, then 0.5, 0.31, 0.209, decaying toward zero.
The long-run cumulative multiplier of variable one onto itself is two and a half, the area under that response.
Because reduced-form shocks are correlated, structural impulses need a Cholesky ordering, and different orderings give different stories.
The variance decomposition then splits each variable's forecast surprise into shares attributable to each orthogonalized shock.
Most economic level series are integrated of order one: they wander like random walks and only their differences are stationary.
Running a VAR on raw levels of two such series invites spurious regression, a high R-squared driven purely by shared trends.
Cointegration is the exception: two integrated series whose linear combination is stationary share a common stochastic trend.
The Vector Error-Correction Model puts the long-run information entirely in Pi, whose rank is the cointegration rank r.
The error-correction term measures distance from equilibrium and pulls each variable back, acting like a thermostat.
The Johansen procedure estimates the rank and cointegrating vectors through trace and maximum-eigenvalue likelihood-ratio tests.
Series x Granger-causes y if the past of x helps predict y beyond what the past of y already explains.
The test fits the y-equation twice and compares an F-statistic between the restricted and unrestricted residuals.

visual.note: does adding x lags improve fit

beats.text: Adding the lags of x cuts the residual sum of squares, and a large enough F rejects the null of no predictive content.
A computed F above roughly 3.9 at the five percent level concludes that x Granger-causes y.
Granger causality is deliberately humble: omitted common drivers or anticipation can produce it with no mechanism running that way.
Tests are sensitive to lag order and non-stationarity, so apply them to differenced data or use the Toda-Yamamoto augmentation.
